section: Understanding and development of residence time distribution (RTD) model in continuous manufacturing

base_metadata.document_title: 2025 J Pharm Inv - Understanding and development of residence time distribution (RTD) model in continuous manufacturing
base_metadata.source_path: /mnt/f_disk/nk/Pfizer_Project_repo/Literature/2025 J Pharm Inv - Understanding and development of residence time distribution (RTD) model in continuous manufacturing.pdf

text: Background In continuous manufacturing (CM), raw materials undergo an uninterrupted transformation, with products that are discharged continuously. A thorough understanding of process dynamics ensures stable operation and effective control. Residence time distribution (RTD) is a crucial tool for characterizing material flow and mixing phenomena within CM. With the increasing adoption of CM, RTD has been extensively studied, proving to be an effective tool for process design, optimization, and control. Area covered This review article provides a comprehensive discussion of the experimental approaches, modeling tech -niques, and applications of RTD methodology in CM. A detailed explanation of the fundamental principles of RTD is provided, followed by an in-depth analysis of measurement procedures, with a particular focus on powder-based systems. Additionally, various applications of RTD in CM processes are explored, highlighting key factors influencing RTD across different unit operations, especially in solid dosage manufacturing. Expert opinion As CM continues to gain traction, RTD has become increasingly valuable in the pharmaceutical industry, demonstrating its utility across various unit operations, including continuous feeders, continuous blenders, twin-screw wet granulators, roller compactors, and tablet presses. In pharmaceutical CM, RTD serves as a valuable tool for material trace -ability, diversion of out-of-specification products, and real-time prediction of product quality. Its application aligns with the principles of Quality-by-Design (QbD) and supports the development of robust control strategies in CM. Moving forward, further advancements in RTD applications are anticipated, enhancing process understanding and strengthening control strat -egies for CM. Keywords Continuous manufacturing (CM) · Residence time distribution (RTD) · Powder behavior · Oral solid dosage manufacturing

text: Continuous manufacturing (CM) has gained significant attention in the pharmaceutical industry due to its numer -ous advantages over traditional batch processing (Lee et al. 2015 ; Burcham et al. 2018 ). In CM, raw materials are con -tinuously fed into the manufacturing process, while finished products are simultaneously removed, with unit operations seamlessly integrated into a continuous workflow (ICH Q13  Hae Woo Lee hwlee@kmedihub.re.kr  1 Drug Manufacturing Center, Daegu Gyeongbuk Medical Innovation Foundation (KMEDI-hub), Daegu 41061, Republic of Korea  2023 ). This advanced processing technology offers several key benefits, including consistent product quality, efficient resource utilization, increased productivity, a reduced manu -facturing footprint, and shorter production cycles (Lee et al. 2015 ; Burcham et al. 2018 ; Su et al. 2019). Another notable advantage of CM is its scalability, as it allows for efficient scale-up to meet production demands with greater ease than batch manufacturing (Maniruzzaman and Nokhodchi 2017). To harness these benefits, CM has been increasingly implemented in the pharmaceutical production of drug substances (Baumann et al. 2020; Hyer et al. 2023) and drug products (Gao et al. 2012 ; Lee et al. 2015 ), demon -strating its potential to enhance productivity and ensure consistent drug quality. In response to these advancements, major regulatory agencies, including the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and European Medicines Agency  (EMA), actively support CM as an advanced manufactur -ing technology aimed at improving drug quality, address -ing medicinal shortages, and accelerating time-to-market (Fisher et al. 2022 ).

text: In response to these advancements, major regulatory agencies, including the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and European Medicines Agency  (EMA), actively support CM as an advanced manufactur -ing technology aimed at improving drug quality, address -ing medicinal shortages, and accelerating time-to-market (Fisher et al. 2022 ). With growing interest in CM, numer -ous studies in recent years have explored its applications across various pharmaceutical manufacturing processes, including chemical synthesis and crystallization (Baumann et al. 2020 ), upstream cell culture and downstream purifica -tion (Godawat et al. 2015 ; Somasundaram et al. 2018) for drug substances, as well as oral solid dosage (OSD) forms (Engisch and Muzzio 2016 ; Furukawa et al. 2020 ; Holman et al. 2021 ) for drug products. Among these, CM has been most extensively applied to the production of OSD forms using techniques such as continuous twin-screw wet granu -lation (Kumar et al. 2013 ) and continuous direct compres -sion (Pauli et al. 2020). CM technologies have also been widely studied for various pharmaceutical formulations, including immediate release (Puri et al. 2017 ), extended or sustained release (Ervasti et al. 2015; Van Snick et al. 2017 ), and solubility-enhancing formulations (Munnangi et al. 2023). Despite significant research efforts, several technologi -cal barriers must still be overcome to ensure the widespread adoption of CM in the pharmaceutical industry. One of the primary challenges is the complexity of process design and control, which is essential for maintaining a process under state of control during continuous operation (Su et al. 2019).

text: Despite significant research efforts, several technologi -cal barriers must still be overcome to ensure the widespread adoption of CM in the pharmaceutical industry. One of the primary challenges is the complexity of process design and control, which is essential for maintaining a process under state of control during continuous operation (Su et al. 2019). CM requires precise control strategies to ensure consistent product quality by adjusting process parameters continu -ously to accommodate potential variations or disturbances (ICH Q13 2023 ). To address these challenges, real-time characterization of product quality and process perfor -mance through on-, at-, or in-line monitoring has emerged as a promising approach. Various process analytical tech -nology (PAT) tools have been successfully integrated into several CM unit operations, with near-infrared spectroscopy (NIR) being widely used to assess blend uniformity, losson-drying, assay, and particle size distribution (Vargas et al. 2018 ; Roggo et al. 2020; Sacher et al. 2022 ). However, implementing PAT presents additional complexities, due to the intricate model calibration, validation, and ongoing maintenance associated with the implementation, making it technically demanding and costly (Brereton 2020). Another constraints associated with adopting CM, par -ticularly in the pharmaceutical industry, are the strict regu -latory requirements and the need for thorough validation of integrated manufacturing processes. While the ICH Q13 guideline (ICH Q13 2023 ) offers comprehensive guidance on the development, implementation, and regulation of CM, there is still uncertainty due to the limited knowledge and experience in this area.

text: Another constraints associated with adopting CM, par -ticularly in the pharmaceutical industry, are the strict regu -latory requirements and the need for thorough validation of integrated manufacturing processes. While the ICH Q13 guideline (ICH Q13 2023 ) offers comprehensive guidance on the development, implementation, and regulation of CM, there is still uncertainty due to the limited knowledge and experience in this area. To address these challenges, effective control strategies must be grounded in a deep understanding of process dynamics, ensuring that products consistently meet quality standards throughout continuous production (ICH Q13 2023). Given these considerations, one of the key parameters that must be characterized in CM is residence time distribu -tion (RTD). RTD defines the time materials spend within a process and provides critical insights into material flow profiles, process dynamics, material traceability, and diver -sion strategies-factors that were less relevant in traditional batch manufacturing (Gao et al. 2012 ). RTD theory has been well-established in the field of chemical engineering since the early 1950s (Danckwerts 1953 ; Fogler 2016) and is widely used to analyze fluid flow patterns, characterize process behavior, and facilitate process scale-up in chemi -cal reactors (Martin 2000 ; Rodrigues 2000 ). However, in pharmaceutical manufacturing, where batch processing has historically dominated, RTD remains a relatively unfamil -iar concept. text: With the increasing adoption of CM, RTD has gained significant interest in the pharmaceutical industry, demonstrating its applicability across various unit opera -tions, including continuous feeders (Bekaert et al. 2021; Bostijn et al. 2019 ; Engisch and Muzzio 2012 ), continuous blenders (Gyurkes et al. 2020 ; Pedersen et al. 2021 ; Lee et al. 2021 ), twin-screw wet granulators (Dahlgren et al. 2019; Thompson 2015 ), roller compactors (Kruisz et al. 2017 ), and tablet presses (Dulle et al. 2018 ; Zimmermann and Thommes 2021 ). In pharmaceutical CM, RTD is a powerful tool for material traceability, diversion of out-of-specifica -tion products, and real-time quality prediction. Its applica -tion aligns with the principles of Quality-by-Design (QbD) and supports the development of robust control strategies in CM (Gao et al. 2012 ; Su et al. 2019; Bhalode et al. 2021). This paper explores the theoretical aspects of RTD in CM, along with methodologies for RTD measurement and its practical applications across various unit operations. Par -ticular emphasis is placed on OSD manufacturing, given the complex nature of solid particle flow in pharmaceuti -cal processes. Through this comprehensive review, we aim to provide deeper insights into material behavior in solidbased CM.

text: In chemical engineering and related fields, RTD is defined as the probability distribution of the time materials spend within one or more unit operations in a continuous process (Danckwerts 1953 ; Fogler 2016 ). RTD is crucial for under -standing the mixing behavior of reactors and processes, especially those that deviate from ideal flow conditions, such as continuous stirred-tank reactors (CSTRs) and plug flow reactors (PFRs). In an ideal PFR, where there is no axial mixing, all material elements spend the same amount of time in the reactor, resulting in a uniform residence time. In contrast, a CSTR exhibits a broader RTD, as incoming feed is fully mixed with the reactor contents before continu -ous effluent removal, leading to a wide distribution of resi -dence times (Fogler 2016 ). In practice, the RTDs of most unit operations fall between these two extremes, depending on the specific mixing characteristics of the process. Experimentally, RTD can be determined by introducing an inert tracer into a unit operation at time t = 0 , and mea -suring its concentration in the output over time, as shown in Fig. 1 . When a pulse of tracer is introduced into the feed, the RTD function is mathematically expressed as the normal -ized tracer concentration in the output (Fogler 2016; Bhalode et al. 2021):  where E ( t ) represents the RTD function, and C out ( t ) denotes the tracer concentration at the outlet.

experimental_setup.detection_method: measuring tracer concentration in the output over time

domain_metadata.key_findings: RTD is crucial for understanding mixing behavior in reactors and processes. Ideal PFR has uniform residence time; CSTR has broad RTD. Most real processes have RTDs between these extremes.

These two parameters are commonly used in RTD analysis to quantitatively compare different RTD pro -files, providing insights into mixing behavior and deviations from ideal flow patterns within a process (Fogler 2016; Lavenspiel 1999). In industrial practice, RTD has long been a fundamental tool for analyzing unit operations across various industries, including petrochemicals, plastics, polymers, food pro -cessing, catalysis, and pharmaceuticals. One of its primary applications is troubleshooting and diagnosing irregular material flow behaviors, such as channeling, bypassing, or dead volumes in reactors (Fogler 2016 ). In chemical reac -tion systems, RTD profiles can also be utilized to estimate reaction yields and conversion rates at the reactor outlet (Lavenspiel 1999 ). Additionally, physicochemical responses obtained through RTD modeling are often integrated into control strategies (Bhaskar and Singh 2019 ), enabling more stable process performance and ensuring consistent product quality.

text: RTD is experimentally determined by introducing a tracer into the system and analyzing its concentration over time. While no regulatory guidelines specify the detailed proce -dures for RTD determination in CM- such as tracer selec -tion, injection method, or data preprocessing-numerous studies have explored the impact of these experimental fac -tors on the resulting RTD profile (Gao et al. 2012; Bhalode et al. 2021). In the literature, pulse and step inputs are the most com -monly used methods for tracer injection, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . In a pulse experiment, a specified amount of tracer is instantaneously introduced into the unit operation at time t = 0 , and its concentration at the outlet is measured over time. The RTD function, E(t) , is then directly computed from the concentration profile using Eq. ( 1 ), providing a simple and effective means of characterizing the process. One key advantage of the pulse method is that it requires minimal tracer compared to other methods, making it par -ticularly beneficial when the tracer is expensive. Theoreti -cally, the duration of the pulse input should be short relative to the residence time of the process, and dispersion at the inlet boundary should be negligible (Fogler 2016 ). How -ever, in many practical applications, achieving these ideal conditions can be technically challenging due to physical limitations. Despite these constraints, pulse testing has been widely applied to various solid processing unit operations in CM, including powder blending, wet granulation, and tab -let compression (Engisch and Muzzio 2016 ; Furukawa et al.

text: How -ever, in many practical applications, achieving these ideal conditions can be technically challenging due to physical limitations. Despite these constraints, pulse testing has been widely applied to various solid processing unit operations in CM, including powder blending, wet granulation, and tab -let compression (Engisch and Muzzio 2016 ; Furukawa et al. 2020 ), due to its simplicity and intuitive experimental procedures. In contrast to the pulse method, the step input approach involves a sudden increase or decrease in tracer concentra -tion in the feed after t = 0 . In this case, the RTD function, E(t) , is indirectly determined by differentiating the concen -tration profile using Eq. ( 3 ). This method can be used as a practical alternative when an instantaneous pulse injec -tion is not feasible, as it does not require an external tracer to induce step changes. However, maintaining a consistent higher or lower tracer concentration throughout the experi -ment can be challenging, and step-up inputs often require a substantial amount of tracer. Additionally, variations in feed composition may significantly alter material flow properties, necessitating careful selection of the step change magnitude (Gao et al. 2012 ). Beyond standard step inputs, alternative approaches such as variable step changes (Bhalode et al. 2021 ) have been explored as extensions to enhance RTD characterization. To conduct RTD experiments effectively, selecting a suitable tracer is essential. RTD behavior is influenced by factors such as system geometry, material properties, for -mulation characteristics, and process operating conditions.

text: Beyond standard step inputs, alternative approaches such as variable step changes (Bhalode et al. 2021 ) have been explored as extensions to enhance RTD characterization. To conduct RTD experiments effectively, selecting a suitable tracer is essential. RTD behavior is influenced by factors such as system geometry, material properties, for -mulation characteristics, and process operating conditions. For example, studies have shown that tracer flow proper -ties-including bulk density, flow function coefficient, and basic flow energy-can significantly impact RTD results. This underscores the importance of ensuring similarity between the tracer and bulk material for reliable experi -mentation (Escotet-Espinoza et al. 2019 ). Although there is no single definitive method for choosing an ideal tracer material, many researchers recommend the following gen -eral guidelines (Deanckwert 1953 ; Fogler 2016 ; Lavenspiel 1999):  - 1) The tracer should be non-reactive with other compo -nents in the feed; - 2) It should have physical properties similar to the bulk material to ensure homogeneous mixing; - 3) It must be easily detectable for accurate quantitative measurement; - 4) It should not adhere to system surfaces, such as walls or processing equipment. Considering the above factors, various tracers are commonly employed in RTD studies, depending on the process type. In many reactors, colored dyes, radioactive isotopes, or inert gases are frequently used due to their effective detection and minimal interference with system dynamics (Fogler 2016; Sheoran et al. 2018 ).

experimental_setup.tracer: colored dyes, radioactive isotopes, inert gases

domain_metadata.key_findings: Tracer properties such as bulk density, flow function coefficient, and basic flow energy significantly impact RTD results. Tracer should be non-reactive, have physical properties similar to bulk material, be easily detectable, and not adhere to system surfaces.

text: Considering the above factors, various tracers are commonly employed in RTD studies, depending on the process type. In many reactors, colored dyes, radioactive isotopes, or inert gases are frequently used due to their effective detection and minimal interference with system dynamics (Fogler 2016; Sheoran et al. 2018 ). For powder-based CM processes, numerous studies have utilized active pharmaceutical ingre -dients (APIs) as tracers, as they allow for seamless imple -mentation without disrupting the flow behavior of the blend (Gao et al. 2012 ). However, other tracers, such as dyes or pharmaceutical excipients, have also been widely used, as illustrated in Table 1 , which provides a comprehensive list of RTD applications along with the tracer types used in the experiments. In a study by Escotet-Espinoza et al. ( 2019 ), various APIs and excipients were categorized into distinct groups based on their material properties. The find -ings revealed that certain grouped tracers exhibited MRTs that differed from those of the bulk blend, highlighting the importance of tracer selection for accurate RTD measure -ment. More recently, Razavi et al. ( 2003) developed an extended data library containing material properties for 162 different tracer species to establish a standardized protocol for tracer selection. Another critical experimental parameter in RTD studies is the amount of tracer introduced into the system. Since adding excess tracer can alter convection and diffusion phe -nomena within the process, only a minimal amount should be used to prevent disturbances in bulk flow behavior.

text: ( 2003) developed an extended data library containing material properties for 162 different tracer species to establish a standardized protocol for tracer selection. Another critical experimental parameter in RTD studies is the amount of tracer introduced into the system. Since adding excess tracer can alter convection and diffusion phe -nomena within the process, only a minimal amount should be used to prevent disturbances in bulk flow behavior. How -ever, the tracer concentration must still be high enough to be reliably detected throughout the experiment. There is no universal standard for the ideal tracer quantity; however, Escotet-Espinoza et al. ( 2019 ) recommended that the mass of tracer material should be kept below 5% of the steady state mass hold-up in pulse tests. Similarly, another study (Pauli et al. 2020 ) found that step inputs in the range of 3-5% were sufficient in accurately determining RTD when using API as a tracer. For measuring tracer concentration at the outlet, both offline and on-line analytical methods can capture the concen -tration profile over time. On-line techniques, such as NIR and Ultraviolet (UV) spectrometry, are often preferred for real-time data acquisition. However, special attention must be given to signal preprocessing and ensuring an appropri -ate quantification range to maintain measurement accuracy (Bhalode et al. 2021).

domain_metadata.key_findings: Tracer mass should be below 5% of steady state mass hold-up in pulse tests. Step inputs in the range of 3-5% are sufficient for RTD determination. On-line techniques like NIR and UV spectrometry are preferred for real-time data acquisition.

text: Once RTD data are collected, data fitting can be per -formed to model the RTD behavior of the given process. A straightforward approach involves empirically fitting the RTD data using well-established probability distribution functions, such as Gaussian or normal distributions (Sen -car et al. 2020 ). This method helps generate a smooth RTD profile by minimizing measurement errors; however, it does not provide deeper insights into process dynamics. There -fore, in many cases, ideal and non-ideal reactor models are employed to characterize flow and mixing behaviors within the system (Fogler 2016). text: In this review, a comprehensive analysis of RTD charac -terization and its application in continuous pharmaceuti -cal manufacturing are provided, with a particular focus on OSD production involving solid particles. While CM is also being implemented in other pharmaceutical areas, such as drug substance synthesis (Baumann et al. 2020) and biologics production (Godawat et al. 2015 ; Somasundaram et al. 2018 ), the focus here is on OSD manufacturing due to the inherent complexity and heterogeneity of material flow and particle interactions. Typical OSD manufacturing processes involve multiple unit operations, including blending, granu -lation, milling, compression, and encapsulation, among oth -ers. To accurately assess process dynamics and their impact on product quality, RTD must be thoroughly characterized not only within individual critical unit operations but also across the entire integrated manufacturing process. The following sections will further elaborate on these aspects, highlighting the role of RTD in optimizing process perfor -mance and ensuring consistent product quality.
section: Application of RTD to pharmaceutical processes

text: In CM, raw materials are continuously fed into downstream unit operations, making precise control over the feed flow rate of each formulation ingredient essential for ensuring potency and content uniformity in both intermediate and final products. To achieve this, loss-in-weight (LIW) feed -ers equipped with screw-based conveying systems are com -monly utilized. These feeders regulate screw speed using either gravimetric or volumetric control modes, adjusting the mass flow rate to match the designated setpoint. Exten -sive research has been conducted on continuous feeder sys -tems, highlighting the challenges associated with cohesive and poorly flowing powders, which can lead to issues such as bridging and rathole formation (Cartwright et al. 2013; Bekaert et al. 2021). While the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the mass flow rate is often used to assess feeder performance (Engisch and Muzzio 2012 ), analyzing RTD provides deeper insights into material flow behavior. By comparing experimental and theoretical RTDs, it is possible to detect flow anomalies such as bypassing or dead zones within the feeder system (Fogler 2016 ), ensuring optimal performance and consistent material delivery. Van Snick et al. ( 2019 ) investigated the RTDs of two dif -ferent screw feeders under varying process conditions, and interpreted the results using a TIS model, where a PFR was followed by a CSTR. Through multivariate analysis, they identified flow rate, hopper level, and material density as critical process parameters and material attributes influ -encing RTD in both feeders. Similar correlations between process and material properties with feeding performance have been observed in other studies (Bekaert et al.

text: ( 2019 ) investigated the RTDs of two dif -ferent screw feeders under varying process conditions, and interpreted the results using a TIS model, where a PFR was followed by a CSTR. Through multivariate analysis, they identified flow rate, hopper level, and material density as critical process parameters and material attributes influ -encing RTD in both feeders. Similar correlations between process and material properties with feeding performance have been observed in other studies (Bekaert et al. 2021; Bostijn et al. 2019 ; Engisch and Muzzio 2012 ; Falk et al. 2015; Santos et al. 2018 ). In addition to experimental RTD studies, computational modeling techniques, such as the discrete element method (DEM) and compartment models, have been employed to predict particle movement within twin-screw feeders. These simulations were used to deter -mine optimal fill levels and refill strategies (Toson and Khi -nast 2019; Waeytens et al. 2022 ; Bhalode and Ierapetritou 2020 ). Especially, refill strategies are important in maintain -ing stable feeder operations. Excessive or infrequent refill -ing of the feeder hopper can lead to significant deviations from the feed rate setpoint, particularly during volumetric feeding mode, where a fixed screw speed delivers a constant material volume (Engisch and Muzzio 2015; Bekaert et al. 2021). By convoluting three indi -vidual RTD profiles, a single RTD for overall process is calculated  The RTD concept is also critical for ensuring the trace -ability of raw materials throughout the CM process, start -ing from the continuous feeder. When different raw material lots are introduced into the system, a specific raw material lot entering the system should be properly tracked along the manufacturing line (Engisch and Muzzio 2016 ; Martinetz et al. 2018 ). By identifying these transitions, manufacturers can effectively manage the risks associated with potential variations in the final product quality (Sanchez-Paternina et al. 2022 ; Karttuen et al. 2019 ; Holman et al. 2021 ). In such cases, RTD analysis provides a robust method for tracking raw materials within the CM process. A practical example of its application can be found in Billups and Singh (2018), where an automated software prototype was developed to trace material flow in a continuous direct compression process, demonstrating the feasibility of real-time material tracking in pharmaceutical manufacturing.

text: Blending is a crucial unit operation in pharmaceutical man -ufacturing, playing a key role in ensuring uniformity of the final product. Unlike batch-based blending, where blend uniformity is assessed only at the end of processing, con -tinuous blending involves a steady discharge of the blended material at the outlet. As a result, a continuous mixer must consistently produce well-mixed blends with minimal fluc -tuations in composition over time. The performance of a continuous blender is influenced by several factors, includ -ing material properties, blender design, and process param -eters, along with the impact of proceeding unit operations, such as continuous feeders (Rogers et al. 2013). In continuous blending operations, the convective mixer is the most commonly used type, where a rotating shaft with an impeller facilitates mixing through convection and dis -persion (Pernenkil and Cooney 2006 ; Portillo et al. 2008). Since RTD characterizes axial dispersion and back-mixing, significant efforts have been made to study RTD in continu -ous powder blending to enhance process understanding and optimize performance (Gyurkes et al. 2020 ; Pedersen et al. 2021 ; Lee et al. 2021 ; Hurley et al. 2022 ). In a continuous mixer, key factors influencing blender performance and RTD include design parameters (e.g., vessel size, agitator geometry, and weir type), process parameters (e.g., impel -ler speed, hold-up, and throughput), and powder proper -ties (e.g., particle size, density, and cohesiveness).

In a continuous mixer, key factors influencing blender performance and RTD include design parameters (e.g., vessel size, agitator geometry, and weir type), process parameters (e.g., impel -ler speed, hold-up, and throughput), and powder proper -ties (e.g., particle size, density, and cohesiveness). Various researchers have explored the relationship between material properties, process parameters, and RTD using statistical techniques such as the design of experiments (DOE), prin -cipal component analysis (PCA), and partial least squares (PLS) (Vanarase and Muzzio 2011; Vanarase et al. 2013; Portillo et al. 2010 ; Pedersen et al. 2021 ). While findings may not be universally applicable, powder density, mass hold-up, and impeller speed have consistently been iden -tified as critical parameters affecting RTD in continuous mixing. In literature, the TIS model is widely used to model RTD in continuous mixing (Gao et al. 2012 ). Studies have shown that the TIS model effectively describes RTD behavior in continuous mixers by fitting experimental data with a mini -mal number of model parameters. Another commonly used approach is the axial dispersion model, derived from the Fokker-Planck equation, which has been applied to powder mixing systems (Schmelzle et al. 2014 ). However, a major limitation of these models is their inability to account for blend heterogeneity within their framework. To address this, a two-dimensional TIS model, incorporating axial and radial mixing, has been recently proposed and experimen -tally validated (Galbraith et al. 2018 ).

Additionally, more complex approaches, such as DEM and population balance models (PBM), have been employed to simulate particle mixing under diverse design and operating conditions (Rog -ers et al. 2013 ; Boukouvala et al. 2013a , b; Sarkar and Wassgren 2009 , 2012; Yang et al. 2023). While these advanced methods offer detailed insights into solid particle flow pat -terns within the mixer, they are often associated with high computational costs and extended simulation times. Insights gained from RTD characterization play a crucial role in equipment design, predicting critical quality attri -butes, and managing out-of-specification products in con -tinuous mixing. For example, Marikh et al. ( 2008 ) evaluated two impeller types under various material flow regimes, analyzing their impact on mass hold-up and mixture homo -geneity. Their study suggested an optimized mixer design that enhanced back-mixing while reducing residence time. Figure 4 presents a funnel plot derived from the RTD function of a typical TIS model, which is then con -voluted with the inlet concentration profile from the LIW feeder to predict the outlet concentration of the mixer. The plot highlights that, within a specific disturbance magnitude and duration, the output concentration remains within the target assay range (100 ± 10%). It is important to note that when blend homogeneity must be monitored, other quality metrics, such as RSD, can also be used to assess variability in composition over time (Pern -enkil and Cooney 2006 ). Overall, this approach facilitates risk-based disturbance management by supporting real-time decision-making during CM operation, allowing for the effective diversion of non-conforming material (ICH Q13 2023).

text: While high-shear and fluid-bed granulation remain standard in the pharmaceutical industry, twin-screw wet granulation (TSWG) has emerged as a key unit operation, enabling the CM of granulated products. Granulation enhances flowabil -ity, compactability, and content uniformity, ensuring that downstream tableting and encapsulation can proceed with -out quality-related risks. In TSWG, a blend is continuously fed into a granulator, where two co-rotating screws generate convective and shear forces, interacting with a binder liquid to form granules with the desired product attributes, such as granule size, porosity, compressibility, and dissolution kinet -ics (Dahlgren et al. 2019 ; Thompson 2015 ). The twin-screw system consists of conveying and kneading elements, whose configuration determines the degree of convective and dis -persive mixing during granulation. Simultaneously, binder liquid is introduced into the granulator barrel via peristal -tic pumps. Improper liquid nozzle design has been shown to cause non-uniform liquid distribution and solid buildup inside the granulator barrel, leading to variations in powder flow and granule properties (Vercruysse et al. 2014 ; Dahl -gren et al. 2019 ). Given that the MRT of TSWG is very short (typically less than one minute) (Kumar et al. 2013 ), a thor -ough understanding of RTD and its impact on granule prop -erties is crucial for ensuring the robust control of continuous granulation processes. for illustration. However, significant efforts have been made to identify critical design variables, material attributes, and process parameters within the framework of QbD (Yu et al. 2014 ). Various granulation phenomena-including wetting, nucleation, consolidation, growth, and breakage-have been investigated under different formulation conditions (El Hagrasy et al. 2013; Meier et al. 2016 ). Additionally, several studies have explored the impact of key process parameters such as powder feed rate (Dhenge et al. 2010 ; Plath et al. 2021 ), screw speed (Thompson and Sun 2010 ; Dhenge et al. 2010 ), liquid-to-solid ratio (Tan et al. 2011 ; Plath et al. 2021 ), binder composition (Thompson and Sun 2010; Tan et al. 2011 ), and liquid feed addition method (Shah 2005) on granule properties and RTD. Given that RTD is influenced by these critical parameters, it is essential-particularly from a regulatory perspective-to characterize RTD com -prehensively within the design space. Given that RTD is influenced by these critical parameters, it is essential-particularly from a regulatory perspective-to characterize RTD com -prehensively within the design space. Interestingly, Ismail et al. ( 2019 ) applied artificial neural network (ANN)-based machine learning techniques to predict the MRT based on process parameters and screw configuration, reducing the reliance on experimental RTD characterization. Among the parameters considered, screw configuration had the most significant impact on MRT, highlighting the need for fur -ther exploration of screw element interactions with both RTD and granule properties. While studies have shown that increasing the number of kneading elements leads to lon -ger residence times, a more systematic approach is needed to evaluate the influence of screw design on mixing and granulation in TSWG. To gain deeper insight, continuous camera measurements (Plath et al. 2021 ) and chemical imaging (Vercruysse et al. 2014 ) can be used to visualize material flow and its associated physicochemical transfor -mations. Alternatively, particle dynamics can be explored using multi-scale simulations based on DEMs and PBMs, as demonstrated in previous research (Arthur and Rahmanian 2024; Zheng et al. 2021; Morrissey et al. 2021).

domain_metadata.key_findings: Screw configuration has the most significant impact on mean residence time (MRT). Increasing the number of kneading elements leads to longer residence times. Artificial neural network (ANN)-based machine learning techniques have been used to predict MRT based on process parameters and screw configuration. A more systematic approach is needed to evaluate the influence of screw design on mixing and granulation in TSWG.

base_metadata.source_path: /mnt/f_disk/nk/Pfizer_Project_repo/Literature/2025 J Pharm Inv - Understanding and development of residence time distribution (RTD) model in continuous manufacturing.pdf

text: In the production of the final dosage form, compaction of either powders or granular blends into tablets is carried out using a tablet press, which operates inherently in a continu -ous mode. As a result, it can be seamlessly integrated into a CM platform. Numerous studies in the literature have explored the applications of continuous direct compres -sion, demonstrating its feasibility and advantages in CM (Jarvinen et al. 2013 ; Simonaho et al. 2016; Van Snick et al. 2017 ; Roth et al. 2017). In a typical rotary tablet press, materials are first intro -duced into the feed frame, where a rotating wheel ensures uniform powder distribution before die-filling (Mendez et al. 2010 ; Ketterhagen 2015 ). The extent of material distri -bution and back-mixing within the feed frame significantly affects tablet weight uniformity. On the other hand, high shear stress and prolonged residence time can lead to overlubrication, resulting in quality defects (De Backere et al. 2022 ). Dulle et al. ( 2019 ) examined the effects of feed frame volume, filling wheel design, and gap size in a productionscale rotary tablet press on the RTD of powder blends. Their findings demonstrated that reducing feed frame volume led to a substantial decrease in both the mean and variance of RTD. Additionally, gap size had minimal impact, offering deeper insights into powder behavior in continuous tablet compression. As seen in other unit operations, the input materials introduced into the tablet press can significantly impact the resulting RTD.

domain_metadata.key_findings: Reducing feed frame volume led to a substantial decrease in both the mean and variance of RTD. Gap size had minimal impact on RTD. Material distribution and back-mixing within the feed frame significantly affect tablet weight uniformity. High shear stress and prolonged residence time can lead to overlubrication and quality defects.

text: Their findings demonstrated that reducing feed frame volume led to a substantial decrease in both the mean and variance of RTD. Additionally, gap size had minimal impact, offering deeper insights into powder behavior in continuous tablet compression. As seen in other unit operations, the input materials introduced into the tablet press can significantly impact the resulting RTD. To explore the relationship between material properties and RTD parameters in a commercial-scale tablet press, Furukawa et al. ( 2020 ) conducted RTD experiments using materials with a wide range of properties, establish -ing a linear correlation between bulk density and MRT. Additionally, they proposed a numerical simulation based on RTD to help select excipients with a lower risk of pro -ducing out-of-specification products. Similarly, Puckhaber et al. ( 2020 ) compared the RTDs of two excipients-dical -cium phosphate and microcrystalline cellulose-across two different tablet presses. Their results confirmed the linear correlation between density and MRT. However, they also observed variation in RTD behavior between the two excipi -ents under different process conditions, indicating that RTD is not only material-dependent but also equipment-depen -dent. These findings highlight the importance of considering interactions among material properties, equipment design, and process conditions when interpreting RTD data. For continuous tablet compression, several research -ers have investigated the effects of powder flow rate, turret speed, fill height, and paddle speed on RTD (Mateo-Ortiz and Mendez 2015 ; Dulle et al. 2018 ; Puckhaber et al. 2020; Zimmermann and Thommes 2021 ).

domain_metadata.key_findings: Reducing feed frame volume leads to a substantial decrease in both the mean and variance of RTD. Gap size has minimal impact. A linear correlation between bulk density and mean residence time (MRT) was established. RTD behavior is both material-dependent and equipment-dependent.

text: These findings highlight the importance of considering interactions among material properties, equipment design, and process conditions when interpreting RTD data. For continuous tablet compression, several research -ers have investigated the effects of powder flow rate, turret speed, fill height, and paddle speed on RTD (Mateo-Ortiz and Mendez 2015 ; Dulle et al. 2018 ; Puckhaber et al. 2020; Zimmermann and Thommes 2021 ). Mateo-Ortiz and Men -dez ( 2015 ) found that higher paddle speeds resulted in a lower MRT, a narrower MRT profile, and a greater num -ber of paddle passes. However, other studies (Dulle et al. 2018 ; Zimmermann and Thommes 2021 ) reported conflict -ing results, noting no significant effect of paddle speed on MRT or paddle passes. This discrepancy may stem from differences in material properties and tracer selection, as illustrated in Puckhaber et al. ( 2020 ), where the relation -ship between paddle speed and MRT varied depending on the material characteristics. However, an increase in tur -ret speed consistently led to a shorter MRT across multiple studies, underscoring its critical influence on the RTD of the continuous tablet compression process. To model the RTD in a tablet press, the TIS model, consisting of a PFR and a CSTR, is commonly employed (Mateo-Ortiz and Mendez 2015 ; Zimmermann and Thom -mes 2021 ). In this approach, the material flow from the hop -per to the feed frame is approximated as plug flow, while the feed frame, equipped with a rotating paddle wheel, is rep -resented as a CSTR.

flow_model.type: TIS model (Plug Flow Reactor + Continuous Stirred-Tank Reactor)

text: To model the RTD in a tablet press, the TIS model, consisting of a PFR and a CSTR, is commonly employed (Mateo-Ortiz and Mendez 2015 ; Zimmermann and Thom -mes 2021 ). In this approach, the material flow from the hop -per to the feed frame is approximated as plug flow, while the feed frame, equipped with a rotating paddle wheel, is rep -resented as a CSTR. The resulting RTD model can then be leveraged for quality assurance, as demonstrated by Bhas -kar and Singh ( 2019 ). Their study proposed an RTD-based control strategy that integrates RTD modeling with PAT to detect and divert out-of-specification tablets during CM. As an alternative RTD modeling approach, several researchers have utilized DEM-based simulations to explore the com -plex particle dynamics within the feed frame (Ketterhagen 2015 ; Mateo-Ortiz and Mendez 2015; Hildebrandt et al. 2019; Mahto et al. 2024 ). For example, these simulations successfully predicted particle segregation during the diefilling process (Mateo-Ortiz and Mendez 2015; Hildebrandt et al. 2019 ), aligning well with experimental findings (Dulle et al. 2018 ) and providing valuable insights into the intricate behavior of materials in continuous tablet compression.

flow_model.type: TIS model (combination of PFR and CSTR)

parameters.CSTR_section: feed frame with rotating paddle wheel

domain_metadata.key_findings: TIS model is used for RTD in tablet press; PFR represents hopper to feed frame flow; CSTR represents feed frame with paddle wheel; RTD modeling integrated with PAT for quality assurance; DEM-based simulations used to study particle dynamics and segregation in feed frame.

text: In CM, all unit operations are interconnected, meaning that any modification in one stage can influence upstream and downstream processes, ultimately affecting product qual -ity (ICH 13 2023 ). Consequently, residence time must be assessed not only at the individual unit operation level but also across the entire manufacturing line. The RTD model for the full process can be derived by convoluting the RTD models of individual unit operations (Engisch and Muzzio 2016 ). Additionally, a combined RTD model for multiple unit operations can be deconvoluted, allowing separation into its individual RTD components, each representing a distinct unit operation (Bhalode et al. 2021 ). The RTD for the entire CM line offers a holistic perspective on process behavior and has been widely applied for material trace -ability (Gao et al. 2012 ; Engisch and Muzzio 2016 ), lot definition (Martinetz et al. 2018 ), product quality predic -tion (Kattunen et al. 2019 ), material diversion (Kruisz et al. 2017; Sacher et al. 2020 ), and continued process verification (Pauli et al. 2020 ). A notable application of RTD in CM is its use in ongoing process monitoring during commercial pro -duction, as demonstrated by Pauli et al. ( 2020 ). Their study introduced low-level step changes in API concentration at regular intervals, continuously tracking the RTD metrics to diagnose process stability and performance. Another widely used approach for incorporating RTD in CM involves the flowsheet model, where a process-wide model is constructed by integrating individual RTD-based models for each unit operation (Boukouvala et al.

domain_metadata.key_findings: RTD models for individual unit operations can be convoluted to derive a full process RTD model. Combined RTD models can be deconvoluted to separate individual unit operation RTD components. RTD is used for material traceability, lot definition, product quality prediction, material diversion, and continued process verification. RTD is applied in ongoing process monitoring during commercial production with low-level step changes in API concentration.

base_metadata.source_path: /mnt/f_disk/nk/Pfizer_Project_repo/Literature/2025 J Pharm Inv - Understanding and development of residence time distribution (RTD) model in continuous manufacturing.pdf

text: Their study introduced low-level step changes in API concentration at regular intervals, continuously tracking the RTD metrics to diagnose process stability and performance. Another widely used approach for incorporating RTD in CM involves the flowsheet model, where a process-wide model is constructed by integrating individual RTD-based models for each unit operation (Boukouvala et al. 2013a , b ; Garcia-Munoz et al. 2017; Metta et al. 2019; Tian et al. 2019 ). These models provide valuable insights into complex upstream and downstream interactions among critical mate -rial attributes (CMAs), critical process parameters (CPPs), and critical quality attributes (CQAs), extending beyond sin -gle-unit operations. Once the flowsheet model is validated, a sensitivity analysis is typically performed to quantify how model inputs impact model outputs, enabling the identifica -tion of key CMAs and CPPs that influence CQAs (Bouk -ouvala et al. 2012 ). Furthermore, the RTD-based flowsheet model can be applied beyond process understanding, sup -porting process design, optimization, and feedback or feed -forward control within a 'digital twin' framework (Ntamo et al. 2022; Moreno-Benito et al. 2022 ). This enables a com -prehensive, real-time view of the entire CM line, enhancing process efficiency, robustness, and control.

text: This review provides a comprehensive discussion of the experimental approaches, modeling techniques, and appli -cations of RTD methodology in CM. To achieve this goal, an overview of RTD fundamentals, followed by a detailed discussion of measurement techniques, is addressed with a particular focus on powder-based systems. While no for -mal regulatory guidelines exist for RTD characterization, key findings and recommendations from various research -ers are summarized to offer a holistic perspective on accu -rate RTD measurement. In addition, the review explores RTD applications across CM processes, highlighting criti -cal factors affecting RTD in each unit operation, particu -larly in solid dosage manufacturing. Despite the extensive literature on RTD applications, challenges remain in tracer selection, data processing, and RTD model validation. Moreover, certain unit operations require a more systematic RTD evaluation to gain a better understanding of material flow dynamics, as interactions between material properties, process parameters, and RTD-dependent quality attributes make data interpretation complex. Nevertheless, RTD meth -odology is gaining wider acceptance in the pharmaceutical industry, with increasing applications in flowsheet modeling and process optimization. Looking ahead, further advance -ments in RTD utilization are expected, solidifying its role as a valuable tool for enhanced process understanding and robust control strategies in CM. text: Guangshuo Wu, Linxi Li, Zhipeng Li, Junhao Wang * and Zhengming Gao *  State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China  * Correspondence: wangjunhao@buct.edu.cn (J.W.); gaozm@mail.buct.edu.cn (Z.G.)
section: Residence Time Distribution of Variable Viscosity Fluids in the Stirred Tank

base_metadata.document_title: 2025 Processes - Residence_Time_Distribution_of_Variable_Viscosity_
base_metadata.source_path: /mnt/f_disk/nk/Pfizer_Project_repo/Literature/2025 Processes - Residence_Time_Distribution_of_Variable_Viscosity_.pdf

text: Stirred tanks are widely used in polymerization processes, where the residence time distribution (RTD) significantly affects monomer conversion and polymer quality. In this study, the RTD in the stirred tank with both constant and variable viscosity fluids was investigated numerically. To account for the viscosity evolution during polymerization, a model relating fluid viscosity to the mean age of the fluid was developed. After verifying mesh and time step independence, the effects of impeller speed, fluid space time, and viscosity varying on RTD were examined in both single-tank and two-tank configurations. Compared to the constant-viscosity fluids, the variable-viscosity fluid shows different flow behaviors such as dead zones and short-circuiting. Analysis based on the number of tanks in series showed that increasing impeller speed and extending space time can enhance mixing efficiency, where the improved mixing in the second stage of the two-tank configuration eliminated the concentration fluctuations caused by recirculating flow in the first tank, which may result in a more uniform RTD curves. Keywords: residence time distribution; stirred tank; variable-viscosity fluids; computational fluid dynamics

domain_metadata.key_findings: Variable-viscosity fluid shows different flow behaviors such as dead zones and short-circuiting. Increasing impeller speed and extending space time can enhance mixing efficiency. Improved mixing in the second stage of the two-tank configuration eliminated concentration fluctuations caused by recirculating flow in the first tank, resulting in more uniform RTD curves.

base_metadata.source_path: /mnt/f_disk/nk/Pfizer_Project_repo/Literature/2025 Processes - Residence_Time_Distribution_of_Variable_Viscosity_.pdf

text: Continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) are widely used in chemical and polymerization processes due to their simple construction, operational flexibility, and excellent mixing performance [1]. In polymerization processes, the performance of reactor is strongly influenced not only by the chemical kinetics but also by the internal flow behavior and mixing efficiency [2]. The residence time distribution (RTD) plays a crucial role in evaluating reactor performance, as it reflects flow characteristics, mixing effectiveness and back mixing degree [3]. Accurate characterization of RTD is essential for predicting monomer conversion, polymer molecular weight distribution, and final product quality in polymerization processes [4,5]. This becomes particularly important in polymerization systems with high and variable viscosity [6], where non-ideal flow behaviors, such as dead zones and short-circuiting, are more likely to occur. Therefore, understanding and controlling the RTD is crucial for the optimal design and scale-up of CSTRs in viscous systems. Currently, research on RTD in CSTRs primarily focuses on the effects of impeller type and operational parameters, such as impeller speed and feed flow rate [7]. Samaras et al. [8] compared the RTD of an axial flow impeller (mixel TT) and two radial flow impellers (Rushton turbine and NS turbine) through experiments and numerical simulations. They found that the axial flow impeller is more likely to cause feed short circuit (>10% of the flow rate)  Academic Editor: Haiping Zhu  Received: 31 August 2025  Revised: 12 September 2025  Accepted: 18 September 2025  Published: 19 September 2025  Citation: Wu, G.; Li, L.; Li, Z.; Wang, J.; Gao, Z.

Moreover, they found that when the ratio of residence time to mixing time is less than 10, the dead zone volume in the reactor increases significantly. In contrast, Jones et al. [9] analyzed the variance of RTD and pointed out that evaluating reactor performance solely based on the ratio of residence time to mixing time could result in over-design and excessive energy consumption. Instead, they proposed that the ratio of the inlet jet momentum to the impeller emission momentum is a more appropriate parameter. They suggested that enlarging the inlet pipe diameter is more energy-efficient than increasing impeller speed in reactors prone to short-circuiting. Aghbolaghy et al. When the ratio of residence time to mixing time is less than 10, the dead zone volume increases significantly. Increasing impeller speed improves mixing but benefits diminish beyond ~375 rpm.

text: They suggested that enlarging the inlet pipe diameter is more energy-efficient than increasing impeller speed in reactors prone to short-circuiting. Aghbolaghy et al. [10] showed that increasing impeller speed improves mixing and approximates ideal behavior, but the benefits diminish beyond a threshold speed (approximately 375 rpm in their study), while energy consumption rises sharply. Bai et al. [11] reported that, without agitation in a three-stage mixing system, the RTD curve exhibited a broad profile with a long trailing tail. With agitation, however, the RTD showed a longer minimum residence time and a significantly shorter tail. Zhang et al. [12] found that whether in a four-stage or a two-stage agitated contactor, increasing the fluid flow rate led to a decrease in mean residence time and deterioration in mixing performance. They also investigated the RTD of sugar solutions at different concentrations, showing that increasing fluid viscosity reduced back-mixing and prolonged the residence time. Hui et al. [13] showed that when the ratio of impeller discharge flow rate to the feed rate exceeded 13.13, the reactor behavior resembled that of an ideal CSTR. When the ratio was below this value, mixing characteristics became highly dependent on the spatial configuration of the inlet and outlet. Although significant progress has been made, most RTD investigations in stirred reactors focus on turbulent flow regimes. The RTD behavior under laminar conditions has been less explored due to experimental challenges.

text: When the ratio was below this value, mixing characteristics became highly dependent on the spatial configuration of the inlet and outlet. Although significant progress has been made, most RTD investigations in stirred reactors focus on turbulent flow regimes. The RTD behavior under laminar conditions has been less explored due to experimental challenges. In such systems, mixing time is often used to characterize the mixing performance [14,15], but its value can vary depending on the chosen definition, even for the same process [16]. In addition to RTD and mixing time, the fluid age distribution is another important parameter for evaluating mixing performance of the reactors. Fluid age is defined as the time elapsed since a fluid particle entered the reactor until it reaches a specific position in the reactor. When the particle eventually exits the reactor, its age equals to its residence time in the reactor [17]. Lui [18] applied steady-state numerical simulations to obtain the mean age field in a stirred tank reactor, which revealed the complex and non-uniform mixing patterns. A corresponding mean age frequency function was introduced, and it was shown that the width of this function is directly related to the mixing time. In a subsequent study [19], the impact of inlet and outlet configurations was examined, showing that shortcircuiting and poorly mixed regions could be effectively identified via mean age contour maps. Under laminar flow conditions, Simcik et al. [20] validated the consistency of RTD and fluid age distributions in a 2D tubular reactor.

domain_metadata.key_findings: Mixing characteristics depend on inlet and outlet spatial configuration. RTD behavior under laminar conditions is less explored. Fluid age distribution is an important parameter for evaluating mixing performance. Mean age contour maps can identify shortcircuiting and poorly mixed regions. RTD and fluid age distributions are consistent under laminar flow in a 2D tubular reactor.

text: In a subsequent study [19], the impact of inlet and outlet configurations was examined, showing that shortcircuiting and poorly mixed regions could be effectively identified via mean age contour maps. Under laminar flow conditions, Simcik et al. [20] validated the consistency of RTD and fluid age distributions in a 2D tubular reactor. Moreover, the concept of fluid age has been extended to other reactor types, including falling film reactors [21] and continuous polymerization reactors [22]. To understand the residence time behavior in viscous systems, this study investigates the RTD characteristics in continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) equipped with a draft tube by numerical simulation. Considering that the fluid viscosity in polymerization processes varies with reaction progress, a viscosity model based on fluid mean age is proposed to account for the viscosity evolution during the reaction. After verifying grid and time step independence, the effects of impeller speed, space time, and viscosity on the RTD are analyzed under both single-stage and two-stage reactor configurations. In particular, the influence of viscosity variation on flow patterns, such as dead zones and short-circuiting, is examined. The study also quantifies mixing performance using the tank in series model. The results demonstrate that considering viscosity evolution is crucial for accurately predicting RTD and guiding the design and scale-up of stirred tank reactors.

text: Numerical simulations were conducted in a flat-bottom stirred tank reactor with a diameter T 0 = 476 mm and a height H = 476 mm. The reactor was equipped with a draft tube with an inner diameter T 1 = 310 mm and a height of 321 mm, which promotes axial flow in the reactor. Five segmented baffles with a width w = 31 mm were evenly distributed inside the draft tube to prevent the fluid whirling in the reactor. A five-blade CBY impeller (a type of impeller with long thin blades) with a diameter D = 300 mm was used in this study, with an off-bottom clearance of 190 mm. The fluid enters the reactor through an inlet tube located on the lower part of the sidewall and exits through an outlet tube located on the upper part of the opposite sidewall. Both the inlet and outlet tube have a diameter d = 20 mm. Simulations were conducted in a single-stage stirred reactor and in two-stage stirred tank reactors in series. The key dimension and three-dimensional structure of the reactors are shown in Figure 1. Figure 1. ( a ) Key dimension of the reactor. text: The step tracer method was used to measure the RTD of the STRs. Once the flow field in the reactor stabilized, the inlet fluid was switched to a tracer with the same physical properties as the original fluid. The concentration of the tracer at the outlet is monitored over time until a stable state was reached. To describe the fluid flow in the STRs, the governing equations of fluid dynamics are employed, including the continuity equation for mass conservation and the Navier-Stokes equations for the momentum conservation. where u is the velocity, ρ is the density, p is the pressure, and µ is the dynamic viscosity. The species transport equation was used to describe the mixing and mass transfer behavior of the tracer. The conservation equation of species j is given by:  where Yj is the mass fraction of species j , Dj is the molecular diffusivity. In the polymerization process, the viscosity of the fluid strongly depends on the conversion. Since the conversion is governed primarily by the reaction time in the reactor, this study relates the fluid viscosity to its mean age in the reactor, which refers to the average time required for the fluid to travel from the inlet to a spatial position in the reactor. When the fluid enters the reactor, its age starts at zero and increases continuously until it exits. When the fluid flows out of the outlet, the age is the residence time in the reactor.

text: In this study, the flow and mixing behavior in the STRs were investigated using the Ansys Fluent 2021 R2. Based on the calculated impeller Reynolds number ReD under the simulated simulations, the flow pattern was determined to be laminar flow. Therefore, the laminar model was used to describe the fluid flow. The computational domain was divided into two parts: a rotating zone and a stationary zone. The rotating zone included the impeller and its surrounding region, while the stationary zone consisted of the remaining part of the reactor. The sliding mesh approach was employed to handle the rotating impeller in transient simulations, whereas the Multiple Reference Frame approach was adopted for steady-state simulations. Date exchange between the two zones was achieved through variable interpolation at the sliding interfaces. All wall boundaries were subjected to no-slip conditions. The pressure-velocity coupling was resolved using the Coupled algorithm. To improve the calculation accuracy, the second-order upwind scheme was used to discretize the governing equations. Convergence was achieved when the residuals of all governing equations fell below 10 -4 . The simulation of RTD involved two steps. First, a steady-state simulation was conducted to obtain a stable flow field. Then, the inlet fluid was switched to a tracer with the same physical properties as the original fluid, and transient simulations were performed. The cumulative residence time distribution function, F ( t ), was determined by monitoring the tracer concentration at the outlet. For numerical simulation, the number of grid cells has a significant impact on the computational process and results.

An insufficient number of cells can lead to significant numerical errors, while an excessive number of cells can reduce computational efficiency. Therefore, grid independence verification is essential. The single-stage STR was meshed with boundary layer grids generated near the impeller to accurately capture the shear flow. text: The effect of impeller speed on RTD under both constant and variable viscosity conditions is illustrated in Figure 6. As the impeller speed increases, the peak of RTD curves appears earlier, indicating an accelerated liquid circulation in the reactor and improved mixing performance. Multiple peaks are observed in RTD curves under all impeller speeds expect at 0 rpm. This behavior is attributed to the circulation flow induced by the draft tube in the reactor. Driven by the impeller, the fluid flows downward inside the draft tube and upward outside, re-entering from the top to form a stable loop. Under laminar flow conditions, the tracer undergoes multiple circulation cycles before exiting the reactor, resulting in multiple peaks in the RTD curves. As the impeller speed increases, the circulation becomes faster, which reduces residence time differences and shortens the intervals between successive peaks. Additionally, enhanced mixing at higher impeller speeds reduces both the number and amplitude of the RTD peaks. In Figure 7, the number of tanks in series is plotted against impeller speed for both constant and variable viscosity conditions. In both cases, the number of tanks in series decreases with increasing impeller speed, except at 0 rpm under variable viscosity.This trend reflects improved mixing performance, which gradually approaches that of an ideal reactor. However, at higher impeller speeds, the rate of improvement diminishes due to limitations in mixing enhancement under laminar flow. At 0 rpm, the number of tanks in series is less than 1 under variable viscosity conditions, indicating the presence of dead zones or short-circuiting flows. Figure 6.

text: The effect of space time on RTD was studied by varying the inlet velocity of the reactor, and the results are shown in Figure 9. With increasing space time, the peaks in the RTD curves are delayed and reduced in height, indicating a longer average residence time. This leads to a large difference in residence times along different flow paths, resulting in a broader RTD curve and more significant tailing. For example, under constant viscosity conditions, at a space time of 45 s, the RTD curve ( E ( t )) becomes nearly zero around 200 s, indicating nearly all fluid has been replaced by the tracer. At a space time of 360 s, the RTD curve remains non-zero even at 500 s, reflecting incomplete fluid replacement. Figure 9. Effect of space time on RTD in single-stage STR: ( a ) constant viscosity of 10 Pa · s; ( b ) variable viscosity described by Equation (19) ( N = 80 rpm). Furthermore, as shown in Figure 9, doubling the space time generally reduces the RTD peak height by approximately half, except for the case of variable viscosity at a space time of 45 s. In this case, a sharp early peak is observed, indicating short-circuiting flow. This is confirmed by Figure 10, where the number of tanks in series is less than 1. Due to the higher inlet velocity and non-uniform viscosity distribution, a part of fluid bypasses the main circulation and exits the reactor quickly, resulting in a narrow residence time distribution for this part of the fluid.

Variable viscosity at τ = 45 s shows a sharp early peak, indicating short-circuiting flow.

Due to the higher inlet velocity and non-uniform viscosity distribution, a part of fluid bypasses the main circulation and exits the reactor quickly, resulting in a narrow residence time distribution for this part of the fluid. This finding highlights the importance of selecting appropriate space time to suppress short-circuiting and ensure effective mixing in polymerization processes. Figure 10. text: Polymerization processes are typically characterized by significant changes in viscosity in the reactor. Figure 11 illustrates the effects of both constant and variable viscosity on RTD. Figure 11. Effect of viscosity on RTD in single-stage STR: ( a ) constant viscosity; ( b ) variable viscosity ( N = 80 rpm, τ = 180 s). For constant-viscosity fluids, increasing viscosity causes the RTD peaks to appear later and grow taller. This is because higher viscosity increases the resistance to flow, reduces the circulation induced by the impeller, and prolongs the time required for the tracer to travel from the inlet to the outlet. Under such conditions, the impeller-driven circulation becomes less effective, resulting in weaker mixing and greater concentration of the tracer around a specific residence time, which produces a sharper and narrower peak in the RTD curve. In addition, higher viscosity slows down homogenization of the tracer, so that the tracer repeatedly recirculates in the reactor and exits intermittently, leading to multiple peaks in the RTD curve. For fluids with different variable viscosities, the RTD curves show only slight differences among fluids with different viscosities. Compared to the constant viscosity cases, more fluctuations are observed in the RTD curves, indicating poorer mixing performance under variable viscosity conditions. As shown in Figure 12, the number of tanks in series increases with viscosity under constant-viscosity conditions. Under laminar flow conditions, higher viscosity leads to a poorer mixing performance.

domain_metadata.key_findings: Higher viscosity increases resistance to flow, reduces impeller-induced circulation, and prolongs tracer travel time. RTD peaks appear later and grow taller with increasing viscosity. Poorer mixing performance under variable viscosity conditions. More fluctuations observed in RTD curves for variable viscosity fluids. Number of tanks in series increases with viscosity under constant-viscosity conditions.

text: Compared to the constant viscosity cases, more fluctuations are observed in the RTD curves, indicating poorer mixing performance under variable viscosity conditions. As shown in Figure 12, the number of tanks in series increases with viscosity under constant-viscosity conditions. Under laminar flow conditions, higher viscosity leads to a poorer mixing performance. As viscosity increases from 1 Pa · s to 500 Pa · s, the number of tanks in series rises from 1.20 to 1.36, representing an increase of only 13%, indicating that viscosity has a weaker influence on RTD compared to impeller speed or space time. For variable-viscosity fluids described by Equations (12)-(14), the corresponding numbers of tanks in series are 1.19, 1.17, and 1.20, respectively. As shown in Figure 5, for mean ages below 112.5 s, the viscosity follows the order: Equation (21) > Equation (19) > Equation (20). Accordingly, the number of tanks in series increases in the same order, reflecting that higher viscosity reduces mixing efficiency and broadens the RTD. Figure 12. Effect of viscosity on the number of tanks in series in single-stage STR ( N = 80 rpm, τ = 180 s).

domain_metadata.key_findings: Higher viscosity leads to poorer mixing performance and broader RTD. Viscosity has a weaker influence on RTD compared to impeller speed or space time. Variable-viscosity fluids show reduced mixing efficiency and broader RTD compared to constant-viscosity fluids.

base_metadata.source_path: /mnt/f_disk/nk/Pfizer_Project_repo/Literature/2025 Processes - Residence_Time_Distribution_of_Variable_Viscosity_.pdf

text: Based on the results and discussion in Section 3.1, the variable-viscosity model, which accounts for the increase in fluid viscosity over residence time, provides a more accurate prediction of flow behavior in polymerization reactors than the constant-viscosity model. Simulations using the variable-viscosity model revealed abnormal flow behaviors. Therefore, in the analysis of residence time distribution (RTD) in two-stage STRs, only variable-viscosity conditions were considered, as simulations under constant-viscosity conditions would not provide additional meaningful insights.

domain_metadata.key_findings: Variable-viscosity model provides more accurate prediction of flow behavior in polymerization reactors than constant-viscosity model. Simulations using variable-viscosity model revealed abnormal flow behaviors. Constant-viscosity simulations do not provide additional meaningful insights in two-stage STRs.

text: Figure 13 shows the residence time distribution of fluid viscosity described by Equation (19) under different impeller speeds in two-stage STRs. The results indicate that the effect of impeller speed on RTD is relatively limited except at 0 rpm. At 0 rpm, the peak in the RTD curve appears later and is significantly higher, which is consistent with the behavior observed in the single-stage reactor. This behavior indicates the formation of substantial dead zones in the absence of agitation. Compared to the single-stage reactor, the RTD curves of the two-stage reactor are smoother and show few fluctuations. This behavior may be attributed to the addition mixing provided by the second reactor, which enhances the dispersion of the tracer from the first reactor and reduces local concentration peaks. Figure 13. Effect of impeller speed on RTD in two-stage STRs with variable viscosity described by Equation (19) ( τ = 180 s). As the impeller speed increases, the corresponding numbers of tanks in series are 1.82, 2.31, 2.21, and 2.20, respectively. Except for the 0 rpm case, the decrease in the number of tanks in series with increasing speed reflects enhanced mixing performance. At 0 rpm, the number of tanks in series is lower than the actual number of tanks, further confirming the presence of dead zones. Figure 14 shows the distributions of viscosity and tracer concentration in the two-stage STRs at t =1000 s. Dead zones are observed in both reactors. However, their extent is significantly reduced compared to the single-stage configuration.

domain_metadata.key_findings: At 0 rpm, the RTD peak is later and higher, indicating dead zones. The two-stage reactor shows smoother RTD curves due to enhanced mixing from the second reactor. The number of tanks in series decreases with increasing impeller speed (except at 0 rpm), indicating improved mixing performance. Dead zones are present but reduced compared to single-stage reactors.

Figure 14 shows the distributions of viscosity and tracer concentration in the two-stage STRs at t =1000 s. Dead zones are observed in both reactors. However, their extent is significantly reduced compared to the single-stage configuration. This result demonstrates that multistage reactor configurations can improve mixing performance and reduce stagnant regions. Figure 14. The viscosity distribution ( a ) and mass fraction of tracer distribution ( b ) in the two-stage STRs at t = 1000 s ( N = 0 rpm, τ = 180 s, viscosity described by Equation (19)).

text: The influence of space time on RTD in two-stage STRs is shown in Figure 15. As space time increases, the RTD peaks are delayed and reduced in magnitude, while the overall curve becomes broader. This indicates an increase in average residence time and a more uniform residence time distribution, which corresponds to improved mixing and flow behavior approaching that of an ideal stirred reactor. The increasing tailing of the RTD curves with space time further supports this observation. The decrease in the number of tanks in series also reflects enhanced mixing with longer space times. These trends are consistent with previously reported RTD characteristics in multistage agitated contactors [12]. Figure 15. Effect of space time on RTD in two-stage STRs with variable viscosity described by Equation (19) ( N = 80 rpm).

domain_metadata.key_findings: As space time increases, RTD peaks are delayed and reduced in magnitude, and the overall curve becomes broader, indicating increased average residence time and more uniform RTD. Tailing of RTD curves increases with space time, suggesting improved mixing and flow behavior approaching that of an ideal stirred reactor. The number of tanks in series decreases with longer space times, reflecting enhanced mixing.

text: As shown in Figure 16, viscosity has a negligible effect on the RTD in two-stage STRs. The RTD curves under different viscosity conditions are nearly identical, and the corresponding number of tanks in series show minimal variation. This phenomenon can be attributed to three main factors. First, the differences among the three viscosity models used in this study are relatively small. At the same mean age, the maximum viscosity difference is around 30 Pa · s, and the viscosities become identical when the mean age exceeds 112.5 s. Second, as discussed in Section 3.1.3, the RTD characteristics of STR are not significantly affected by viscosity. Third, the presence of the second stirred tank enhances mixing and further mitigates the effect of viscosity differences. Consequently, the influence of viscosity on RTD in two-stage STRs can be considered negligible. Figure 16. Effect of viscosity on RTD in two-stage STRs ( N = 80 rpm, τ = 180 s).

domain_metadata.key_findings: Viscosity has a negligible effect on RTD in two-stage STRs. RTD curves under different viscosity conditions are nearly identical. Maximum viscosity difference is around 30 Pa · s at the same mean age. Viscosities become identical when mean age exceeds 112.5 s. The presence of the second stirred tank enhances mixing and mitigates viscosity effects.

text: In this paper, a simplified viscosity model based on the mean age of the fluid was proposed to describe viscosity evolution during polymerization. The residence time distribution (RTD) of the Newtonian fluids and the variable-viscosity fluids in the stirred tank reactors equipped with the draft tubes was investigated numerically. The results demonstrated that considering viscosity evolution is essential to capture abnormal flow behaviors such as dead zones and short-circuiting, underscoring the importance of incorporating viscosity variation for accurate RTD prediction in polymerization reactors. The study also showed that impeller speed and space time are the dominant factors controlling the RTD in laminar stirred tanks, whereas viscosity has a relatively minor effect. The comparison between single- and two-stage configurations revealed that the second stage significantly improves mixing uniformity, smooths RTD curves, and mitigates the influence of viscosity differences. Beyond reproducing flow characteristics, these findings emphasize the significance of the proposed simplified viscosity model. By linking viscosity to the mean age of the fluid, the model provides an efficient way to capture the effect of viscosity evolution on RTD without introducing excessive complexity. This approach provides practical guidance for reactor design, scale-up, and operation in polymerization processes. Since the effect of shear stress on viscosity was not considered, the proposed model is applicable mainly to Boger-type fluids, which are insensitive to shear stress. In future work, we will extend the model to incorporate the effect of shear stress on viscosity, in order to more accurately capture viscosity variations in polymerization reactors.

domain_metadata.reactor_geometry: stirred tank reactors equipped with draft tubes

parameters.viscosity_evolution: based on mean age of fluid

domain_metadata.key_findings: Considering viscosity evolution is essential to capture abnormal flow behaviors such as dead zones and short-circuiting. Impeller speed and space time are dominant factors controlling RTD in laminar stirred tanks. Viscosity has a relatively minor effect. The second stage significantly improves mixing uniformity, smooths RTD curves, and mitigates the influence of viscosity differences. The proposed simplified viscosity model provides an efficient way to capture the effect of viscosity evolution on RTD without excessive complexity.
